answer:
**No direct match exists in Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation, Vol. I* for Johann Gottlieb Fichte's explicit claim—advanced in *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (ch. 2)—that freedom of thought is an inalienable right preceding and constraining legitimate civil authority.[^1][^2]**

Fichte roots legitimate rule in a social contract alienating only *permitted* (not commanded) external actions; freedom of thought, as a core of personhood essential to morality and reason, cannot be alienated and thus limits princely power, which derives solely from society (itself so limited).[^3] Princes may protect rights but never suppress thought-communication, as society lacks authority to demand such renunciation.[^4]

Comte, by contrast, analyzes authority as composed of observable "elements of force" (opinions, passions, physical sanctions of influential classes/princes), distinguishing *arbitrary regimes* (undescribed, irregular power) from *legal regimes* (pre-described actions, yielding uniformity—but no inherent goodness).[^5] Submission to either is mere "obedience to necessity," not guaranteeing liberty or justice; even "legal" power oppresses if malevolent.[^6] He rejects mythical contracts/general will (chs. 6–7) as veiling arbitrary force, insisting legislation be fact-based observation of human nature/effects—but nowhere privileges thought-freedom as logically prior/constraining.[^7][^8]

Both constrain "legitimacy" (Comte via empirical regularity/analysis; Fichte via inalienable rights/contract limits), rejecting Rousseauvian fictions enabling despotism. Yet Comte's focus is power's *composition/effects*, not Fichte's *thought-right precedence*.[^9] The corpus lacks "freedom of thought"/"liberté de penser" in Vol. I; nearest are general liberty critiques, unmatching Fichte's claim.[^10]

[^1]: J. G. Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit...*, ch. 2 [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-e62dae).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-900390](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-900390), [p-b77ba3](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-b77ba3).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-7062a4](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-7062a4), [p-ff7538](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-ff7538).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-be3fae](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-be3fae).

[^5]: C. Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, ch. 13 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-72aa45).

[^6]: Ibid., [p-fadb97](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-fadb97), [p-9f5b65](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-9f5b65).

[^7]: Ibid., ch. 6 (full) [chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/06-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-civil/translation/).

[^8]: Ibid., ch. 7 (full) [chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/07-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-comme/translation/).

[^9]: TF-IDF links chs. 6–7 to Fichte ch. 2 (cosine ~0.32), but via shared anti-Rousseauvian themes, not thought-freedom.

[^10]: Corpus-wide searches ("freedom of thought" OR "liberté de penser" OR Denkfreiheit + authority/rule) yield 0 hits in Vol. I.
